source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Identify how sounds are made, associating some of them with something vibrating; recognise that vibrations from sounds travel through a medium to the ear; find patterns between the pitch of a sound and features of the object that produced it; find patterns between the volume of a sound and the strength of the vibrations that produced it; recognise that sounds get fainter as the distance from the sound source increases.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack uses safe virtual instruments, visible vibration models and distance observations to connect sound evidence with vibration, pitch, volume and travel through materials.

objective.child_goal: I can explain how sounds are made, travel and change.
objective.statement: Explain that sounds are made by vibrations that travel through a medium to the ear, and use observations to compare pitch, volume and distance effects.
misconceptions: Thinks sound is made only when an object is hit, not when it vibrates.
Thinks sound can travel through empty space in the same way as through air or solids.
Thinks sounds disappear suddenly rather than becoming fainter with distance.

vocabulary.audio_script: A vibration is a quick back-and-forth movement that can make sound.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A ruler twanging over the edge of a table with a motion trail.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A material that vibrations can travel through, such as air, water or a solid.
vocabulary.audio_script: Sound vibrations travel through a medium to reach your ear.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Vibration rings moving through air particles between a drum and an ear.

vocabulary.child_explanation: How high or low a sound is.
vocabulary.audio_script: Pitch tells us whether a sound is high or low.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Short and long strings labelled high pitch and low pitch.

vocabulary.child_explanation: How loud or quiet a sound is.
vocabulary.audio_script: Volume tells us whether a sound is loud or quiet.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Small and large vibration traces from the same drum.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The object or place where a sound begins.
vocabulary.audio_script: The sound source is where the vibration begins.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A bell highlighted as the source with sound rings spreading out.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Find what is moving when each sound is made.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Activate observation of the sound source rather than guessing from the object name.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Look for the part that vibrates. That movement is the clue.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A tuning fork, ruler, drum skin and vocal cords are shown before and after making sound.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sounds are made when something vibrates.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Establish vibration as the cause of sound using visible, safe examples.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: When the object vibrates, it pushes nearby material back and forth. Those vibrations can reach your ear as sound.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A slowed-down vibration model links object movement to spreading sound waves.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Trace the vibration journey from the source, through the air, to the ear.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model sound travel through a medium without overloading learners with advanced wave physics.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The object vibrates, the air passes the vibration along, and the ear detects it.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Particle dots pass a gentle pulse from a bell to an ear diagram.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Change one feature at a time. Does the sound become higher, lower, louder or quieter?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Separate pitch from volume through controlled virtual instrument observations.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Pitch is high or low. Volume is loud or quiet. Change one thing and record what happens.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A virtual string, ruler and drum show vibration speed/length and vibration size as evidence.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Move the listener away from the source. What pattern do you notice in the volume reading?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect distance from source with sounds becoming fainter.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: As the listener moves farther away, the sound usually becomes fainter.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A sound-level meter records three distances from the same steady source.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach your companion why a high sound is not always a loud sound.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair pitch-volume confusion through explanation from evidence.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I thought high pitch meant loud. Which observation proves they are different?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two sounds are compared: high quiet whistle and low loud drum.

manipulatives.purpose: Reveal the vibrating part of familiar sound sources and link visible movement to heard sound.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners select a source, start/stop the sound, use slow-motion view, then identify the vibrating part and evidence.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Every sound has captions, waveform-free child-friendly visual traces and a silent mode; vibration is shown with clear motion trails, tactile-style icons and still before/after frames.

manipulatives.purpose: Separate pitch from volume by changing one instrument feature at a time and recording observations.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners adjust string length, ruler overhang, pluck strength or drum tap strength, then classify the effect as pitch or volume.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: No sudden loud audio; default audio is gentle and optional. Reduced-motion mode uses paired still traces and all controls are keyboard reachable.

manipulatives.purpose: Observe the pattern that sound becomes fainter as the listener moves farther from a steady source.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners place listener markers at measured distances, run the same source and compare meter readings and written observations.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Distance markers include numbers and spacing, not colour alone; a table equivalent is provided for the visual field.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave vibration-source identification with pitch, volume and distance tasks only after learners can explain that the source is vibrating.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Identify the vibrating part of familiar sound sources using observation evidence.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: sound source + slow-motion clue + part selection + evidence sentence
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use safe classroom examples only; avoid implying children should place objects in ears or make unsafe loud sounds.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Trace vibrations from source through air, water or a solid to a listener.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: source + medium + listener + choose/complete travel path + explain medium
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep the model qualitative; do not introduce vacuum demonstrations as classroom activities.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Find patterns between pitch and features such as length, tightness or size of the vibrating object.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: instrument feature comparison + high/low prediction + observation match + reason
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Teacher review must check that each instrument pattern is scientifically appropriate for Year 4 simplification.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Connect louder sounds with stronger vibrations while distinguishing volume from pitch.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: same source + gentle/strong vibration evidence + loud/quiet choice + because frame
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid using distressing loudness cues; present volume visually and with safe optional audio.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Use distance observations to explain why sound gets fainter farther from the source.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: same source + three listener distances + meter readings + pattern conclusion
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Do not overclaim exact inverse-square relationships; keep to observable Year 4 pattern language.

body.prompt: A ruler is pulled down and released over the edge of a table. What is making the sound?
observations: The ruler moves quickly up and down
The sound stops when the ruler stops moving
choices: The ruler vibrating
The table staying still
The air disappearing
The ruler changing colour
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

expected_answer.value: The ruler vibrating

hints: Look for the part that moves quickly back and forth.
What happens to the sound when the movement stops?
question_variants.explanation: The sound is made by the ruler vibrating. When the vibration stops, the sound stops.

feedback.correct: After the response is checked, the accepted answer is value: "The ruler vibrating". The sound is made by the ruler vibrating. When the vibration stops, the sound stops.
feedback.repair: Look for the part that moves quickly back and forth. What happens to the sound when the movement stops? Use that clue to repair sound without vibration, then try the same decision again.
feedback.evidence: For this vibration observation, use the observable feature, ordered model, comparison or test result rather than a guess. The scientific evidence supports value: "The ruler vibrating": The sound is made by the ruler vibrating. When the vibration stops, the sound stops.
feedback.support_message: Use the picture, model or observation and respond by touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or pointing, or direct an adult to record the choice. Touching materials, fine dragging, handwriting and speech are optional. Retry without a timer, loss or penalty.

choices: The bell vibrates, air passes the vibrations along, and the ear detects them
The bell sends tiny pieces of metal into the ear
The sound jumps through nothing
The ear makes the bell vibrate after hearing it

body.prompt: Two identical strings are plucked with the same strength. The shorter string makes a higher sound. Which property changed?
choices: Pitch
Volume
Distance
Medium
body.supported_interaction: select and place cards using taps, numbered positions or step controls; dragging is optional. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

expected_answer.value: Pitch

hints: High or low describes pitch.
Loud or quiet describes volume.
question_variants.explanation: The shorter string makes a higher-pitched sound. Pitch describes how high or low a sound is.

body.prompt: The same drum is tapped gently, then tapped more strongly. The stronger tap makes a larger vibration trace. What changes?
observations: The drum skin vibrates in both tests
The stronger tap has a larger vibration
The drum is the same size
choices: The sound is louder
The sound must be higher pitched
The sound travels through no medium
The drum stops being the source
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. question_variants.explanation: A stronger tap makes the drum skin vibrate more strongly, so the sound is louder.

choices: Sound gets fainter as the distance from the source increases
Sound gets louder as the distance from the source increases
Distance changes pitch but not volume
The buzzer stops vibrating at 3 m

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks sound is made only when an object is hit, not when it vibrates.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner names the action or object but not the vibrating part, or says sound happens without movement.
repair_sequence: Freeze the source before sound and after sound starts.
Show a slow-motion view of the vibrating part.
Stop the vibration and ask what happens to the sound.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner identifies the vibrating part in a new sound source and links stopped vibration with stopped sound.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks sound can travel through empty space in the same way as through air or solids.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses a path with no medium or says sound jumps straight from source to ear.
repair_sequence: Trace the source, medium and ear as three required parts of the journey.
Compare sound travelling through air with a string telephone model.
Ask learner to label the medium in a new scenario.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains that vibrations travel through a material such as air, water or a solid.

misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner uses loud/quiet to answer high/low questions or says a louder sound is always higher.
repair_sequence: Show a high quiet sound and a low loud sound side by side.
Sort observations into pitch words and volume words.
Change one control at a time and record whether pitch or volume changed.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner correctly classifies high/low as pitch and loud/quiet as volume in two new examples.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks sounds disappear suddenly rather than becoming fainter with distance.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner predicts the same volume at all distances or says the sound source stops when the listener moves away.
repair_sequence: Keep the source constant and move only the listener.
Record three distance and volume observations in a table.
Ask learner to state the pattern using farther and fainter.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner uses comparative distance evidence to explain that sound becomes fainter farther from the source.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Keep audio optional, quiet by default and never startling; provide visual vibration traces for every sound.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace wave motion with step-by-step still panels, labelled arrows and user-controlled reveals.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Offer narrated prompts and replay, but do not require learners to identify sounds by hearing alone.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use short labels, icons for high/low and loud/quiet, and sentence frames such as 'The ___ vibrates, so ___.'.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Lock unused instrument controls and show one variable-change question at a time.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow pause, replay and table review before choosing; remove speeded listening tasks.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Frame wrong predictions as investigation clues and preserve correct observations during retries.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach vibrate, source, medium, ear, pitch, volume, high, low, loud, quiet, near and far with paired visuals.
adaptive_support.hearing_support: Every task is fully solvable from labelled vibration models, tactile-style or physical-token alternatives and text tables. Audio is optional, gentle, replay-controlled and never the only source of evidence.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires identifying vibrating sound sources, tracing sound through a medium and distinguishing pitch from volume in at least two formats. Secure requires using observation evidence to explain vibration strength, pitch patterns and fainter sound with distance across retained sessions.
evidence.parent_summary: Can explain that sounds come from vibrations, travel through materials to the ear and change in pitch, volume and apparent loudness with distance.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Records source observations, medium tracing, variable-control decisions and evidence explanations separately so teachers can see whether vocabulary is grounded in investigation.
next_actions: Route pitch-volume errors to paired high/quiet and low/loud comparisons.
Route source errors to slow-motion vibrating-part identification.
Use retrieval that alternates source, medium, pitch, volume and distance evidence.
Avoid real loudness activities for learners with sound sensitivity; keep virtual audio optional.

qa.notes: Review-stage Year 4 sound pack with a deterministic 240-item pilot bank and five preserved curated variants. The progression covers vibration, sound travel through air, water and solids, pitch, volume, distance, pattern finding, fair tests, ear safety and named misconceptions. Generated candidates include SEND multimodal, tactile and text alternatives, supported non-drag interactions, investigation feedback and untimed sound-lab missions. Independent science, teacher, accessibility, safeguarding, audio-safety and renderer review remain required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
